The problem involves two dormitories named Oakwood and Maplewood.
We know that the total number of students is ninety-six.
This total remains constant regardless of any student movement.
Five students moved from Maplewood to Oakwood recently.
After this move, both dorms have the exact same count.
We need to find the original number in Oakwood.
Let us define the final number in each dorm as X.
Since there are two dorms with equal numbers, the sum is two X.
Two X equals ninety-six students in total.
Dividing ninety-six by two gives us forty-eight.
So each dorm has forty-eight students after the move.
Oakwood gained five students during this relocation event.
Therefore, Oakwood must have had fewer students before.
We subtract five from the current forty-eight count.
Forty-eight minus five equals forty-three students.
This means Oakwood started with forty-three residents.
Maplewood must have started with more than forty-three.
Maplewood lost five students to reach forty-eight.
So Maplewood originally had fifty-three students.
Adding forty-three and fifty-three gives ninety-six.
The math checks out perfectly for the total sum.
The difference between the two original counts is ten.
Moving five students reduces that difference by ten.
This confirms the logic of the relocation process.
